summary: Gruber documents his frustrating experience trying to switch between two iPhones with iPhone Mirroring on his Mac. The setting is buried in an unintuitive location — System Settings → Desktop & Dock under 'Widgets' — rather than in the iPhone Mirroring app itself. He criticizes Apple's inconsistent support documentation and Siri's inability to provide a correct answer, with Siri even fabricating a nonexistent 'list of available iPhones' UI element. The post serves as both a practical how-to and a pointed critique of Apple's discoverability failures across settings, documentation, and AI assistance.
key_points: The setting to switch between multiple iPhones for iPhone Mirroring is buried under Desktop & Dock → Widgets in System Settings, not in the iPhone Mirroring app
Apple's own support documents are inconsistent — one explains multi-iPhone mirroring, another doesn't mention it at all
Siri with Apple Intelligence fabricated a plausible-sounding but completely fictional answer about a 'list of available iPhones' that doesn't exist
Apple Intelligence's 'product knowledge' trained on Apple's own support docs still can't provide accurate answers about Apple's own features
Bluetooth flakiness prevented the multi-iPhone option from even appearing, requiring a toggle off/on to fix
money_quotes: I never would have thought to look in System Settings → Desktop & Dock, let alone, even if I happened to look in that panel, all the way down under 'Widgets'.
No wonder Apple Intelligence's 'product knowledge', trained on these documents, is somewhat confused.
This answer certainly describes one possible way that using multiple iPhones with iPhone Mirroring *should* work, but as an answer for how it actually *does* work, it's abject nonsense.
There is no 'list of available iPhones' in the iPhone Mirroring app. If there were such a list to choose from, I'd never have had a question about this whole fucking thing in the first place.
I bet the *next* version of Bluetooth is actually going to be reliable.
He also has expressed a dim view of chatbots in the past, telling Apple employees before and immediately after the release of ChatGPT that he didn't believe they added much value for users.
key_insight: Apple's AI assistant confidently fabricated a nonexistent UI feature rather than admit ignorance, perfectly illustrating why hallucinating LLMs make poor product support tools — especially when the underlying documentation they're trained on is itself inconsistent.
